A man of Cyrene, in Jerusalem for the Passover. The soldiers seized me out of the crowd and forced me to carry the crossbeam behind Him up to Golgotha. I did not choose that road, but I have never been the same, and neither have my sons.

A criminal, justly condemned, crucified at His right hand. The other mocked Him; I said, 'We deserve this, He has done nothing wrong.' Then: 'Jesus, remember me when You come into Your kingdom.' He told me: 'Today you will be with Me in paradise.' 🌅
A criminal crucified beside Him. I joined the mockery: 'Aren't You the Christ? Save Yourself, and us!' My companion rebuked me. I died with a sneer on my lips.

A Roman centurion, in command of the execution detail at Golgotha. I have watched many men die. But when this one breathed His last, the darkness, the torn veil, the earthquake, I said what I knew to be true: 'Truly this man was the Son of God.'

A respected member of the council, from Arimathea, and a secret disciple, for fear of the others. I did not consent to what they did. So I went boldly to Pilate and asked for His body, and laid Him in my own new tomb, cut from the rock. Nicodemus came with the spices.

Servant of Caiaphas the high priest. When they came to arrest the Galilean in the garden, that fisherman Peter drew a sword and cut off my right ear. Then the prisoner Himself touched it, and healed me. I will never forget it.

On the third day I was walking to Emmaus with a friend, heartbroken, we'd hoped He was the one to redeem Israel. A stranger joined us and opened the Scriptures till our hearts burned. Then He broke the bread, our eyes opened: it was Him! And He vanished. We ran the seven miles back to Jerusalem that night. 🔥
Wife of Clopas, one of the women who stood by the cross when most had fled, and came to the tomb with spices at first light. We found the stone rolled away, and everything changed.
Chosen by lot to take Judas's place among the Twelve, I had followed Jesus from John's baptism to the ascension, an eyewitness like the rest. Now I serve quietly among the apostles.

A Levite of Cyprus, they call me 'son of encouragement,' and I try to earn it. I sold my field for the church, vouched for Saul when everyone else feared him, and gave young John Mark a second chance after Paul gave up on him. Somebody has to believe in people.

A believer in the early days, when no one claimed anything as their own. My wife and I sold a field, and quietly kept back part of the price while pretending we'd given it all. Peter saw through it. I never left that room alive.

Wife of Ananias. We agreed together on the lie about the land money, and when Peter asked me, I told it again to his face, not knowing my husband already lay dead. 'How could you test the Spirit of the Lord?' Three hours, and I followed him.

A Pharisee, a teacher of the Law honored by all the people, and once the teacher of a young firebrand named Saul. When the council wanted the apostles dead, I counseled patience: if this is of men it will fail, but if it is of God, you cannot stop it, you might find yourselves fighting God.

A man full of faith and the Holy Spirit, chosen to serve the widows, but I could not keep silent. Hauled before the council on false charges, I told them the whole story of God's faithfulness and their hard hearts. As the stones flew I saw heaven open: 'Lord Jesus, receive my spirit. Do not hold this sin against them.' 🪨

One of the seven chosen to serve tables, but God sent me preaching. I took the gospel to Samaria, then a desert road, where I met an Ethiopian official puzzling over Isaiah in his chariot. I climbed up, explained the Scripture, baptized him on the spot, and the Spirit whisked me away.

A court official of Candace, queen of the Ethiopians, keeper of all her treasure. I'd been to Jerusalem to worship, and was reading Isaiah on the road home: 'like a lamb led to slaughter.' A man named Philip ran up and told me it was Jesus. 'Look, water! What prevents me from being baptized?' Nothing did. I went home rejoicing. 💧

A sorcerer of Samaria, they once called me 'the Great Power of God.' When I saw the apostles give the Spirit by laying on hands, I offered them silver to buy the gift. Peter rebuked me sharply: 'Your money perish with you.' (My name lives on in the word 'simony,' and not in a good way.)

Once Saul of Tarsus, a Pharisee of Pharisees who hunted the followers of the Way and held the coats while they stoned Stephen. Then on the Damascus road, a light, a voice: 'Saul, Saul, why do you persecute Me?' Three days blind, then baptized, and everything reversed. Now I'll cross the empire, shipwrecks, prisons, beatings and all, to preach the One I once hated. ✝️

A disciple in Damascus. The Lord told me in a vision to go lay hands on Saul, the very man who'd come to arrest us! I argued. But I went, said 'Brother Saul,' and scales fell from his eyes. Sometimes obedience means walking straight toward your fear.

A disciple in Joppa, Dorcas, in Greek. I spent my days sewing tunics and garments for the widows and the poor. When I fell sick and died, they sent for Peter; he knelt, prayed, and said, 'Tabitha, arise.' And I opened my eyes. 🧵

A centurion of the Italian Regiment in Caesarea, a Roman, a God-fearer who prayed and gave alms. An angel told me to send for Peter; God gave Peter a vision of a sheet of unclean animals. He came to my house, a Jew, into a Gentile's home!, and the Spirit fell on us all. The door is open to the nations now.

King over Judea, grandson of Herod the Great. I had James the brother of John killed with the sword, and seized Peter too, though he slipped my prison. Later, robed in silver at Caesarea, I let the crowd hail me as a god. I did not give God the glory. They say worms did the rest.

A servant girl in the house where the church was praying for Peter in prison. When he knocked at the gate, I knew his voice and was so overjoyed I ran to tell everyone, and left him standing outside! They said I was out of my mind. Then they opened the door. 😅

Cousin of Barnabas. I set out with Paul and Barnabas, then turned back partway, and Paul didn't forgive that easily; it split the two of them. But Barnabas gave me another chance, and in the end even Paul called me useful. (I also wrote down Peter's memories of the Lord, into a Gospel.)

A leading man among the brothers in Jerusalem, and Paul's companion after he and Barnabas parted ways. I was beaten and chained with Paul in the Philippian jail, and at midnight we sang hymns, and the earthquake shook the doors open. A Roman citizen myself, as it happens.

A young disciple of Lystra, my mother Eunice a Jewish believer, my father a Greek. Paul took me as his own son in the faith, and I've traveled and suffered with him since. He tells me not to let anyone look down on my youth. I try. 🙏

A dealer in purple cloth from Thyatira, living in Philippi, a worshiper of God. By the river on the Sabbath, Paul spoke, and the Lord opened my heart. I was baptized with my whole household, and made the apostles stay at my home. The first believer on European soil, they say. 💜

Keeper of the prison in Philippi. When the earthquake threw the doors open, I drew my sword to kill myself, better than Rome's punishment, but Paul cried out, 'Do yourself no harm, we are all here!' Trembling, I asked, 'What must I do to be saved?' That night my whole house was baptized, and I washed their wounds.

A tentmaker, with my husband Aquila, we left Rome when Claudius expelled the Jews, and met Paul in Corinth; we share the trade and the faith. When we heard the eloquent Apollos preaching only part of the story, we took him aside and explained the Way more fully.

A Jew of Pontus, a tentmaker, husband of Priscilla. We work side by side stitching tents and hosting the church in our home, city to city, Rome, Corinth, Ephesus. We risked our necks for Paul, and we'd do it again.

A Jew of Alexandria, eloquent, mighty in the Scriptures, and on fire, though at first I knew only the baptism of John. Priscilla and Aquila set me straight, and now I water what Paul plants. Some say 'I follow Paul,' others 'I follow Apollos', please don't. We're servants of the same Lord.

A silversmith of Ephesus, I make shrines for Artemis, and good money at it, until this Paul started turning people from idols made with hands. I gathered the trade and stirred the city: 'Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!' Two hours they roared it in the theater.

A young man of Troas. Paul preached on and on past midnight, and I, perched in a third-story window, fell fast asleep, and then just fell. They picked me up dead. But Paul went down, embraced me, and I was alive after all. (I'll never live down falling asleep on the apostle Paul.) 😴

A prophet from Jerusalem. I foretold a great famine in the days of Claudius, and it came. Later I took Paul's belt, bound my own hands and feet, and warned: so the Jews will bind the man who owns this belt. He went to Jerusalem anyway.

Roman governor of Judea. They brought Paul before me, and I kept him two years, partly hoping for a bribe, partly because his talk of righteousness and the judgment to come frightened me. 'Go away for now; when I have time I'll send for you.' I never did.

Successor to Felix as governor. Paul's case baffled me, religious quarrels, nothing worthy of death, and when he appealed to Caesar, I had to send him to Rome. As he reasoned, I shouted, 'Paul, you are out of your mind! Your great learning is driving you insane!'

King, great-grandson of Herod the Great, expert in Jewish customs. Festus brought Paul before me to make sense of his case. The prisoner reasoned so powerfully I said, half in jest: 'In a short time would you persuade me to become a Christian?'

A physician, and a Greek, the only Gentile, they say, to write Scripture. I traveled with Paul (the 'we' in the story is me), and set out to write an orderly account of all Jesus did and taught, then a second volume on how the church spread to Rome. I talked to the eyewitnesses. I wanted Theophilus to know the certainty of it. 🩺

A Greek, a son to Paul in the faith, he wouldn't even let them circumcise me, to make a point about grace. He left me on Crete to set the churches in order and appoint elders, among people he himself called 'always liars, evil beasts, lazy gluttons.' Thanks, Paul. I love it here.

A believer of Colossae, the church meets in my house. My runaway slave Onesimus turned up converted under Paul's care, and Paul wrote asking me to take him back not as a slave but as a beloved brother. (How do you say no to that letter?)

Once a slave in Colossae, my name means 'useful,' though I ran away and was anything but. I met Paul in his chains, and Christ in Paul, and now I go back to my master Philemon carrying a letter that calls me his brother. Useful at last. ⛓️
A servant, a deacon, of the church at Cenchreae, and a benefactor to many, including Paul. He trusted me to carry his letter all the way to the believers in Rome. Quite a journey for quite a letter.

Grandmother of Timothy. A sincere faith lived in me, then in my daughter Eunice, and now in the boy, three generations, from my knee to Paul's right hand. We taught him the holy Scriptures from infancy.

Mother of Timothy, a Jewish believer married to a Greek. My mother Lois and I raised my son in the Scriptures, and when Paul came through Lystra, Timothy was ready. I gave my boy to the mission, and to God.

Of Colossae, a servant of Christ. I brought the gospel to my city, and I wrestle in prayer for those believers always, that they may stand mature and fully assured. Prayer is hard work; I do it anyway.

The church at Philippi sent me to bring their gift to Paul in prison and to care for him. I nearly died of illness doing it, homesick, and worried they'd heard I was sick. Paul calls me his brother, fellow worker, and fellow soldier. I'll take it.

I was a fellow worker with Paul, my name once stood in his letters beside Luke's. But I'll be honest: I loved this present world. In the end I deserted him and went off to Thessalonica. A cautionary tale, I suppose. I had every chance.

A beloved brother and faithful minister. Paul trusted me to carry his letters to Ephesus and Colossae and to tell the churches how he was doing. A reliable pair of feet and a steady heart, that's a ministry too.
When Paul was a prisoner in Rome and others were ashamed of his chains, I searched the whole city until I found him, and I was not ashamed. He often refreshed me; I tried to return the favor.

A woman of Philippi who labored side by side with Paul in the gospel. There's been a... disagreement between me and Syntyche, and apparently it's gotten back to Paul, because he put it in a letter for the whole church to read aloud. Mortifying. 🙈
A fellow worker of Paul's at Philippi. Euodia and I are both in the book of life, Paul says, he just wishes we'd agree with each other. We're working on it. (Slowly.)

A leader in a certain church, and I like to be first, I won't deny it. When the apostle John wrote, I refused to acknowledge his authority, talked wicked nonsense against him, and put out anyone who welcomed the traveling brothers. My house, my rules.

'Most excellent Theophilus', that's how Luke addressed the two volumes he wrote for me, that I might know the certainty of the things I'd been taught. I'm still reading. It's quite a story, and he assures me every word was carefully checked.

A faithful witness in Pergamum, where they say Satan has his throne. I would not deny the Name, and it cost me my life. The risen Christ Himself remembered me by name in His letter to my church: 'Antipas, My faithful witness, who was killed among you.'

They call me a Jezebel, a hard name to carry. I taught in the church at Thyatira, and the risen Christ's letter named me a false prophetess leading His servants astray, and gave me time to repent. The warning was not gentle.
Herdsman in the land east of Eden. Lost two goats to a lion this week and the grass is thin. My cousins in the city laugh at us shepherds, but who feeds them? Honest work, hard land.
Dyer and cook in the city of Enoch. Today's lentils came out incredible 😋 secret is the wild garlic from the riverbank. ALSO: fresh madder-root reds in the dye pots, come see before they're gone! 🔴

Young, and this city never sleeps. Another long night of music and wine in Enoch, Lamech's people really know how to host. The elders keep muttering about 'the wickedness of the age.' Lighten up, old men. 🍷
Midwife among the people of Seth. Three healthy babies this moon, the LORD is good. If you're expecting, send for me, day or night. Blessings on your households. 🤍

Old as the hills, nearly. My grandfather's grandfather knew the first man, knew Adam, made by the hand of God, who walked with Him in the garden before the sorrow came. We have forgotten too much. Sit, and I'll tell you how it was.
Potter in Enoch. New batch of water jars fired clean, not a crack. Trade you a jar for grain or good wool. A pot well-made outlives the potter, my mother used to say.

Granary-keeper in Enoch. So the old shipwright is STILL building that monster boat on dry land, still hollering about a 'flood.' 🙄 It has never once rained like that. Some people will believe anything. Anyway, harvest was good, come buy grain.
Farmer, of the sons of Cain. Cursed ground is what we work, thorns and thistles and sweat. Third poor harvest running. The city folk don't know real toil. (At least the figs came in this year.)
Hunter. Took down a wild ox this morning before most of you were even awake, biggest in the valley, ask anyone. Nobody in these parts hunts like me. 💪
New mama among Seth's people 🥹 the little one is finally sleeping through the night. Motherhood is the hardest, sweetest thing. Any tips for sore feet and no sleep? Send help (and stew). 👶
Piper. Learning the reed-flute from old Jubal's students, music is the closest thing to heaven we've got down here. Playing at the harvest gathering tonight, come find me. 🎶

Laborer on the great ark, yes, THAT ark. People mock us every single day, but old Noah is the only truly righteous man I know, and I'd rather be inside that boat than out of it. Pitched the lower deck today. My hands are wrecked. 🔨
Herdsman in the camp of Abram. We follow the man and his God across this land, tent to tent, well to well. He treats his servants well, and the flocks are fat this season. Praise be.

A girl of Haran, by the well. Rebekah is my dearest friend, and you will NOT believe it, a stranger's servant turned up with ten camels and gold, and now she's leaving to marry a man she's never even met, off in Canaan! 😱 So happy for her. Going to miss her terribly.

Trader at the city gate. Spices from the south, copper from the hills, fine linen, name it, I'll get it. Best prices in Canaan (don't tell my competition). The patriarchs' caravans have been very good for business.

My father keeps the well, so I keep the news 💁 everyone passes through eventually. Heard there's strife AGAIN between Abram's herdsmen and Lot's over the watering rights. The land's just not big enough for both, if you ask me.

A servant in a great house in Egypt. We hear the Hebrews come down when the famine bites, strange customs, but their God seems to look after them. I keep my head down and my eyes open. There's always talk in the kitchens.

Shepherd. If your flock drinks at MY well again, herdsman, there will be words, and maybe more than words. A man's water is a man's life out here. (Abram and Lot had the right idea: separate the camps before blood is spilled.)

Camel-driver on the long roads, Haran to Egypt and back, dust in my teeth the whole way. I've seen every city worth seeing. Ask me anything about the trade routes; I've got stories that would curl your beard.

An old servant in Abraham's house. I was there the night the LORD took him outside and said, 'Look now toward heaven, and count the stars, so shall your offspring be.' Mark my words: a great nation will rise from this old man and his promised son. I won't live to see it. You might.

Midwife to the women of Abraham's camp. When old Sarah laughed at the very thought of a baby, well, I've delivered a few 'impossible' ones in my time. Send for me when your time comes, and don't be afraid. 🤍

A man of Gerar. These wandering Hebrews and their wells, they dig, we argue, we dig again. King Abimelech says make peace with them, their god is clearly with them. Fine. But this is OUR land, and don't forget it.
Weaver and tentmaker in Abraham's camp. New goat-hair panels woven for the big tent before the cold sets in. Slow work, good work. A strong tent is a happy household.
New father, out in the Canaan hills 👶 named him after my own father. Already dreaming of teaching him the flocks and the stars. They grow up in a blink, the elders say. Exhausted, but happy.

Brickmaker in Goshen, like my father before me. They've doubled our quota, make the bricks, find your own straw. My back is breaking and my children are afraid. But old Hodesh swears deliverance is coming. I want so badly to believe him. 🧱

Wife of Eziah, mother of two in Goshen. I hide my little ones and I pray. Strange things keep happening to Egypt, the river turned to blood, then frogs, now a darkness you can feel. The women are whispering that our God has finally remembered us. 🤍

I'm seven! My abba makes bricks and my imma makes the BEST flatbread. Tonight we're painting our doorpost RED and eating super fast with our sandals ON which is so weird but Imma says it's important 🐑🩸
Eziah and Tovah's girl. Imma says we're leaving Egypt TONIGHT and I can only bring what I can carry. I'm bringing my doll and my little goat. Are we really going to see the wilderness?? 😮

An old man of Israel, keeper of the old promises. Joseph made us swear it on his deathbed: 'God will surely visit you, and you shall carry up my bones from here.' Four hundred years we have waited in this land. Children, the day is near. I can feel it in my bones.

Taskmaster over the Hebrew brick gangs. Pharaoh wants the quotas met, straw or no straw. These slaves grumble about their god, let their god make bricks, then. (Though I'll admit... these 'plagues' are beginning to unnerve even me.)

A scribe of Pharaoh's court. I keep the records, and lately the records are nothing but horror: the river is blood, the cattle dead, hail and locusts stripping the land, three days of darkness you could touch. I write it all down with a trembling hand. What god does this? 📜

A young mother of Egypt. They are saying that tonight a plague comes for the firstborn, and my little boy is my firstborn. I am sick with fear. The Hebrews mark their doors with blood and are passed over. I have begged my husband to do the same. 😢

An Egyptian servant girl who works beside the Hebrew women, Tovah is my friend, whatever our peoples say to each other. I have seen their God's power with my own eyes. When they go, I am going with them. There's a 'mixed multitude' leaving Egypt, and I will be in it.

Charioteer of Pharaoh's army. The slaves have fled to the sea and we ride to drag them back, six hundred chariots thundering behind us. The water stands up like walls and they walk through on dry ground. We follow them in. (I have a very bad feeling about this.)
Water-carrier in Goshen. Whatever else happens, somebody still has to haul the water. I've packed my yoke and buckets for the road, wherever this wilderness leads us.
A Hebrew shepherd, slave to an Egyptian lord. My flocks were small and my hopes smaller, until now. They say we leave at dawn with all our herds. I never thought I would live to see freedom.

A weaver of Israel. As we left, the Egyptian women pressed gold and silver and fine cloth into our hands, glad to be rid of us. I have never held such wealth. Part of me is thrilled; part of me wonders what good gold is in a desert. 💰

Out of Egypt, into the desert. Now let me tell you about the FOOD situation. Manna for breakfast. Manna for lunch. Manna, somehow, for dinner. 🙄 Day after day after day. I'm not saying I want to go back to slavery, but I AM saying I miss seasoning. #wildernessproblems

I was at the sea when the waters crashed down on Pharaoh's army, and when Miriam took up her tambourine, I sang and danced with all the women of Israel. 'Sing to the LORD, for He has triumphed gloriously!' I will never forget that song as long as I live. 🥁

A craftsman among the people. They've called for gold, blue and purple yarn, acacia wood, fine linen, we are building a dwelling for God Himself, to His exact design, under Bezalel. The honor of it humbles me. I measure twice and cut once.

I'll be honest about something I'm not proud of. When Moses was up the mountain so long, I was in the crowd that gave Aaron our gold for the calf. I danced before it. Then Moses came down... I have never been so ashamed. The LORD was merciful. I won't forget it.

Mother of three, all born in the wilderness, they've never seen Egypt, never made a brick. They know only manna, the cloud by day, the fire by night, and the tabernacle in the middle of the camp. Maybe that's the better inheritance.

Forager and fixer. Out before the sun every morning to gather the manna before it melts, double on the sixth day, none on the Sabbath, that's the rhythm. I've got the timing down to an art by now. Ask me for tips.

I'll say what half the camp is thinking: I MISS the food in Egypt. The fish we ate for free, the cucumbers, the melons, the leeks and onions and garlic. 😩 Now there's nothing for our eyes to look at but this manna. (Yes, yes, I know how that sounds.)

A Levite, assigned to the service of the camp. We take down and carry the tabernacle when the cloud lifts, and pitch it again when the cloud settles. Holy work, heavy work. When the silver trumpets sound, we move.

Sandal-mender by trade, though honestly? Forty years out here and our sandals haven't worn out and our clothes haven't frayed. Worst possible market for a cobbler. 😅 The LORD provides, I suppose, even when it's terrible for business.

Young, and DONE wandering in circles. We were meant to be in the promised land by now, milk and honey, they said. Instead, sand. My parents' generation lost their nerve at the border. I swear I won't lose mine.

Married my love last new moon, right here in the wilderness, under the stars and the cloud of glory. No fine house, no dowry of fields, just a tent, a future, and a God who feeds us bread from heaven. It is enough for me. 💍

I make and mend the shofars and serve as a camp crier. When the silver trumpets call the assembly, gather, or break camp, that's me passing the word, tent to tent. A loud voice is a calling, my mother always said.

Young, fit, and hoping to be chosen as a scout when it's time to spy out the land. I want to be the one who climbs the hill and sees Canaan first. Caleb and Joshua are my heroes, men who believed when everyone else trembled.

I keep the water skins for my family. There have been stretches, Marah, Rephidim, with no water at all, and the whole camp turned on Moses. Then water came from a rock. I never let our skins run dry now if I can help it. The desert teaches you fast.

Herdsman, I brought my cattle out of Egypt and kept them alive across this whole wilderness, which is no small thing. Good grazing is scarce; I know every green patch within a day's walk of the cloud. Trade you milk for grain.

A young Levite, learning the music of the sanctuary, the cymbals, the harp, the songs. One day, they say, there will be a temple and a great choir. For now I practice in the shadow of the tabernacle and I dream.

Did you HEAR?? 👀 They say the ground opened up and swallowed Korah and his whole rebellious lot, tents and all. And before that, Moses' own sister struck with leprosy for speaking against him! I don't start the rumors, I just... keep everyone informed. 💁

An old man, and I'll speak plainly: I do not believe my generation will ever see this promised land. We doubted at the border, and the LORD swore we would wander until we are all in the ground. Forty years. I've made my peace. May my grandchildren cross over.

A young man of Israel, sword newly in hand. My father wandered forty years and died in the desert; I get to cross the Jordan and take the land. We marched around Jericho in silence, seven days, no one understood why. On the seventh, we shouted. ⚔️

Wife of Othni. While the men take the towns, we women begin to build a real life in this land, a real house, a real field, after a lifetime of tents. I plant my first fig tree this week and I could honestly weep. 🌱
Othni and Bashel's boy. We crossed a RIVER that stood up in a heap so we could walk over on DRY GROUND! And then the walls of a whole city fell DOWN flat! Best year of my whole life. 🏞️

An old man of Israel, one of the few who left Egypt as a child and lived to cross over. We carried Joseph's bones the whole way, four hundred years of a promise kept, and today we buried him at Shechem among his own people. Joseph, you're home. We made it.

A woman of Jericho. When the walls fell, my whole city perished, but I was in the house of Rahab, under the scarlet cord, and we were spared. I serve the God of Israel now. I don't understand all His ways, but I am alive, and grateful.

A man of Gibeon. I'll confess it, when Israel was winning every battle, we put on moldy bread and worn-out sandals and tricked Joshua into a treaty, pretending we'd come from far away. It worked! Mostly. Now we chop their wood and carry their water forever. Could be worse, we're alive. 😅
A woman of Gibeon. My people made a deal, our lives for our labor. So I draw water for the house of the LORD now, day in and day out. Honest work for a second chance. I'll take it.

A scout for Joshua. I was one of the two who slipped into Jericho before it fell, hid on Rahab's roof under the drying flax. I've seen the inside of these cities. Send me ahead; I'll bring back the truth.

Did you SEE it?? ☀️ The sun STOPPED in the sky over Gibeon, just hung there while we finished the battle. A whole extra day of daylight! I keep telling people and they keep saying I exaggerate. I do NOT exaggerate.

I knew Achan. Worked beside him. When Israel lost at Ai and we found he'd hidden plunder from Jericho under his tent, gold, silver, a fine robe, and it cost good men their lives... I'll never forget that valley where it ended. One man's sin, all our trouble.

A stonemason. After a lifetime making bricks for OTHER people's cities, I finally build in a land that's ours. New walls, a new well, a threshing floor. My hands know this work. It still feels like a dream.

A woman of Canaan. These Hebrews and their God sweep through the land like a flood, city after city. Some of us fight, some flee, some make terms. I keep my children close and my head down, and pray to whatever gods will still listen.

An old farmer of Canaan. My fathers worked this valley for generations, and now strangers divide it by lot among their tribes. I am too old to fight and too rooted to run. History never asks the likes of me what we want.

Newly married, settling our first home in the hills of Canaan. A real house with a roof, not a tent that flaps all night! I'm learning to grind grain and keep a hearth. Tired, happy, a little overwhelmed. 🏡

A trumpeter of Israel. Seven priests, seven rams' horns, seven times around the city on the seventh day, then every trumpet and every throat at once. I have never made a sound like that, and the walls came DOWN. My ears still ring. 📯

A farmer in the hill country, when I can farm. The Midianites swarm in at harvest like locusts and strip us bare, so I thresh my little wheat hidden in a winepress, like a thief in my own field. How long, O LORD? (They say a man named Gideon is asking the same.) 🌾

Wife of Ravan. We hide our grain, our goats, and ourselves when the raiders come. I've buried our few valuables three times this year. But there's talk of a deliverer rising up. I light the lamp each night and I hope.

Son of Ravan. They called for fighters, and after a strange test with torches and clay jars and just three hundred men, somehow I'm one of them! We smashed the jars, blew the horns, and the whole Midianite camp turned on itself in the dark. I'll be telling this story till I'm old. 🔥
A woman of Israel in the days of Deborah. A PROPHETESS judges us under her palm tree, and a woman drove the tent peg that ended the great Sisera! Let no one tell my daughters what they cannot be. 🌴

An old man who remembers when we feared the LORD. Now every generation forgets faster than the last, they chase the Baals, get conquered, cry out, get rescued, and forget again. Round and round. 'Everyone does what is right in his own eyes.' It grieves me to my bones.

A Philistine farmer near Timnah. My neighbor is that Israelite strongman, Samson, and let me tell you, life near that man is NEVER dull. Foxes with torches in my grain, a thousand of our soldiers down with the jawbone of a donkey... I just want a quiet harvest. Is that too much to ask?? 🦊

A woman of the Sorek valley. Everyone's talking about Samson and which of OUR girls has caught his eye now. The man cannot resist a Philistine woman, and it never ends well, for anyone. I just listen at the well and keep my opinions to myself. (Mostly.)

Oppressed by foreigners AGAIN? Must be a day ending in 'day.' 🙄 Honestly, at this point I've lived under Moab, under Canaan, under Midian, I've lost track. Wake me when the next judge shows up. I'll be in the cave with everyone else.

I just keep the town informed, that's all 💁 Did you hear Ehud got the fat king of Moab ALONE and, well. Did you hear Jael invited Sisera in for warm milk and a nap? These judges, I tell you, you could not make it up.

A young mother in the hill country, raising little ones in unsettled times. Raiders one season, peace the next, you never know. I teach them the old stories of the Exodus and pray the LORD raises up someone to save us before they're grown.

A son of Midian, and the eastern desert is ours to roam. When the crops ripen in the lands to the west, we sweep in on our camels and take what we please, they cannot stand against us. (Though lately there's a new boldness in those hill-farmers. We shall see.)

A weaver in Ephraim. Good wool, fair prices. In peaceful years the trade is steady; in the bad years, nobody buys anything but bread and blades. I've learned to save in the fat times. The cycle always turns.

Craftsman, and business is BOOMING, because everyone wants a household god these days. Carved or cast, you name it. A little silver one for the shelf, a bigger one for the courtyard. The old folks scowl, but a man's got to eat. (Is it wrong? I try not to think about it.)

Young and restless. I want to follow a real deliverer, a Gideon, an Ehud, a Jephthah, and DO something, instead of hiding in caves while my people suffer. When the next one rises and blows the horn, I'm there.

A vinedresser. My vines don't care who's oppressing us this decade, they need pruning either way. Some years the raiders take the harvest; some years we drink the wine ourselves. I tend the rows and take what the season gives.

A man of Dan. Our tribe never really got our allotment settled, the Amorites pushed us back into the hills. Now some of us are scouting north, looking for easier land to take. Maybe a quiet town that won't see us coming. A fresh start, if you can call it that.

A Levite, and these are hard days for a man of my calling, no temple, no settled place, the people barely remember the LORD. I wander town to town looking for a household that'll take me in as their priest. It's not what Moses intended. But a man has to live.

Steward in the palace, first King Saul's, now King David's. Do you have ANY idea how hard it is to run a royal household when the king keeps weeping, dancing in the streets, or hiding from his own son? I keep the lamps lit, the bread baked, and my opinions to myself. 🕯️

Servant girl in the palace, and let me tell you, the things I see 👀 The king has HOW many wives now? And the sons don't get along AT ALL. I don't gossip... but if I did, I'd have material for a lifetime. Pour the wine, keep your ears open.

A soldier in King David's army. We've fought Philistines, Ammonites, Arameans, I've lost count of the campaigns and the friends. The king fights alongside us, mostly, and he never forgets a man who stood his ground. Home feels very far away tonight. 🛡️

Wife of Beled, off at the wars again. I run the farm, raise our boy, and listen for news from the front with my heart in my throat. They say King David's men always come home victorious. I just want MY man to come home. 🤍
My abba is a SOLDIER for KING DAVID! When he comes home he tells me about the battles and lets me hold his shield (it's SO heavy). I'm going to be a mighty man too one day. 🛡️

An old shepherd of Bethlehem. I taught a ruddy little boy named David how to work a sling and find the still waters, youngest of Jesse's eight, always singing to that harp. Now they tell me that boy is KING over all Israel. I always knew there was something in him. The LORD looks at the heart.

A woman of Bethlehem. I remember when David was just Jesse's youngest, running in from the fields all dusty. We're famous now, 'the city of David,' they call us! Hard to believe the boy from down the lane wears the crown.

A foot soldier, and I was THERE, in the ranks at the valley of Elah, the day the giant came out roaring for forty days and not one of us dared face him. Then a shepherd boy with a sling... I saw it with my own eyes. One stone. I have never doubted that God since.

A Jebusite, Jerusalem was OUR city on these hills, before David's men came up through the water shaft and took it. He didn't drive us out, though, so I stayed. It's the City of David now, and somehow still home too. Strange days.

A craftsman of Tyre. King Hiram sent us south to build for King Solomon, and WHAT a thing we're building. Cedar from Lebanon, gold over everything, stone dressed so fine you hear no hammer at the site. A temple for their God. I've never worked on anything like it. 🌲

A man of Gath, of the Philistines. I'll say it quietly: that David is the finest warrior of the age, and he lived among us once, in exile from his own mad king. Half of me wants to fight him, half of me wishes he were ours. He's something else.

A woman of Gibeah, loyal to the house of Saul, and I'll not pretend to love this David who sits where my king sat. Saul had his faults, but he was OURS, and his blood deserved better than it got. The north remembers. Not everyone is celebrating.

A shepherd supplying the palace kitchens, the royal household eats a STAGGERING amount of mutton, let me tell you. Steady customer, the crown, I'll give it that. Solomon's table alone could feed a whole town.
A midwife in Jerusalem. The city grows so fast under the kings, new babies every week, new houses climbing the hillsides. I bless each little one and pray they grow up in peace, not war, for once. 🤍

A young harpist. I learned to play listening to the king's own psalms drift from the palace, 'The LORD is my shepherd'... can you imagine writing something like that? I want to play in the house of God when it's finished. 🎵

A weaver, I make fine linen and dyed cloth for the palace and the nobles. Business has never been better; under Solomon, they say silver's as common as stones in Jerusalem, and everyone wants finery. I'm not complaining.

I sell wine in the lower market. These are fat years, peace on every side, trade flowing in, gold pouring through the gates. Even ordinary folk drink well now. 'Every man under his vine and fig tree,' they say. Long may it last. 🍷

Conscripted onto the king's building crews. Glory and gold for Solomon, sweat and sunburn for the likes of me. We haul stone and timber month after month and my back is wrecked. The temple's a wonder, sure. I just wish wonders weren't built on tired men. 😔

A scribe in Solomon's administration. Twelve districts, monthly provisions, tribute, trade with Sheba and Egypt and Tyre, somebody has to write it all down, and that somebody is me. The kingdom runs on ink as much as on swords.

Career soldier. Another season, another border, another campaign. Ammon, Aram, Edom, Philistia, I've bled in all of them. They sing about King David the giant-killer; nobody writes a song about the feet that march twenty miles a day. 🙄 Where's MY psalm?

Newly married, and SO excited, they're finishing the great temple in Jerusalem and the whole nation's invited to the dedication! A house for the LORD, after all the years of just a tent. We're traveling up for the feast. 🎉

A caravan trader. Solomon opened the trade roads and now the whole world comes through, spices, horses from Egypt, gold from Ophir, the Queen of Sheba herself with her camels! A good man to do business under, this king. My purse has never been heavier.

An old man, old enough to remember Samuel the prophet, and the days when we had no king but the LORD. We BEGGED for a king 'like the other nations.' Samuel warned us the cost: our sons, our daughters, our fields, a tenth of everything. We got our king. We also got the bill.

A young mother in Jerusalem, raising my little ones in the king's own city. They'll grow up with the temple on the hill and the ark of God in our midst. Whatever the kings do, I just want my children to know the LORD. That's the whole of it for me.

A fisherman, I supply Jerusalem's markets from the Jordan and the coast through the traders. The city's hunger is bottomless these days, all those mouths in the palace and the garrisons. Good for a fishmonger, hard on the rivers.

A baker by the city gate, best spot for news there is. Everyone stops for bread, and everyone talks. The latest? The king's sons. Always the king's sons. Amnon, Absalom... that family's troubles could fill an oven. I just knead and listen. 🍞

When everything fell apart for me, debts, trouble, nowhere to turn, I went down to the cave of Adullam, where David was hiding from Saul. Hundreds of us did, every man in distress and discontent. He made an army of us misfits. I'd follow him anywhere now.

A Levite, a gatekeeper for the tent that holds the ark of God. I keep the thresholds, guard the holy things, open and close at the appointed times. When the temple's finished they say there'll be thousands of us in courses. For now, I keep my post.

I was in the streets the day they brought the ark up to Jerusalem, and the KING himself danced before the LORD with all his might, leaping and spinning, stripped to a linen ephod like a common Levite! Some thought it undignified. I thought it was the most alive I've ever seen a man. 🎶

Cook in the royal kitchens, and you would NOT believe Solomon's daily provision: thirty measures of fine flour, ten fat oxen, sheep beyond counting, deer, fowl... I help feed a household the size of a town, every single day. My feet have not stopped in years. 🍲

A quarryman. We cut and dress the great stones for the temple far from the site, finished perfect before they're hauled in, so no hammer or chisel is heard on the holy mount itself. Sacred silence, built by very loud, very dusty work. Somebody's got to do it. ⛏️

An old man who holds to the promise. The prophet Nathan brought the king word from the LORD: that David's house and throne would stand FOREVER, that a son of his would reign without end. Forever, mind you. I won't see it fulfilled, but mark me, a greater Son of David is coming. I'd stake my life on it. ✨

A farmer in the northern kingdom, in the days of Ahab and that Sidonian queen. They push Baal on us at every turn, but I have not bowed, and I am not alone. The prophet Elijah says seven thousand of us never bent the knee. We keep the LORD quietly, and we wait. 🌾

Wife of Achiam, in the north. We worship the LORD behind closed doors now, it isn't safe to be known for it, not with Jezebel hunting the prophets. I teach our son the old songs in a whisper. Better days will come. They must.
Achiam and Mira's boy. Abba says we serve the real God, the one who made the rain, not the idols the king's wife likes. But I'm not supposed to say so out loud at the market. It's a secret. 🤫

A priest of Baal, brought south from Sidon with Queen Jezebel's court. We have eight hundred and fifty prophets at the king's own table, the land is ours now. (Though there's an old troublemaker named Elijah who keeps... interrupting. We'll deal with him.)

A noblewoman of Sidon, in the queen's retinue. These Israelites and their invisible, imageless god, so dreary. We've brought color, music, the rites of Baal and Asherah. The king's wife knows how to live. I quite like it here. (For now.)

I was on Mount Carmel. I watched the prophets of Baal cut themselves and cry out from dawn to dusk and NOTHING happened. Then Elijah soaked his altar with water, water! in a drought!, called once, and FIRE fell from heaven and consumed everything. I fell on my face. 'The LORD, He is God!' 🔥

A prophet of the LORD, and I'd be dead but for Obadiah, the king's own steward, who hid a hundred of us in two caves and smuggled us bread and water while Jezebel hunted us down. We pray for the day we can come out into the light again.

A young man in the company of the prophets, a disciple of Elisha. I've seen things you wouldn't believe, an axe head float, a poisoned spring healed with salt, a dead boy breathe again, a Syrian general's leprosy washed clean in the Jordan. I follow that man and I learn. ✨

A soldier of Aram, in Damascus. My commander Naaman went to Israel a leper and came back with skin like a child's, and worse, came back worshiping THEIR god, hauling Israelite dirt home to pray on. Strange business. But I cannot argue with what I saw.

Citizen of the glorious northern kingdom, where we've had, let me count, Jeroboam, Nadab, Baasha, Elah, Zimri (seven days, that one!), Omri, Ahab... and I have genuinely lost track since. New king again? Another coup? I keep my head down and pay whoever's collecting taxes this month. 🙄

Samaria's worst-kept secret keeper 👀 Did you HEAR the queen had Naboth STONED just to steal his vegetable garden for the king?? And that Elijah turned up to curse them for it?! This palace, I swear, you could not invent it. Tell no one I told you. (Tell everyone.)

A merchant in Samaria, the capital Omri built on his hill. Ivory, oil, fine wares, business is decent when the kings aren't getting us into wars with Aram, which is... often. I sell to whoever's buying, palace or prophet.

A young woman of the north, and it's hard. My husband's family keeps a Baal shrine and expects me to bow to it, but my heart belongs to the LORD my mother taught me. I pray quietly, and I'm so tired of being afraid. I hope Elijah is right that we're not alone.

I lived in Samaria when the Assyrians came. Three years they besieged us; then the walls broke, and they took us, marched us in chains to far-off lands, scattered us among the nations. The northern kingdom is GONE. We did not listen to the prophets. Now I write to you from exile. 💔

A mother in Jerusalem, in the southern kingdom. We've had good kings and wicked ones, a Hezekiah who tore down the idols, a Manasseh who filled the city with them. I just keep my household faithful through all of it, and pray the line of David holds. 🤍

Husband of Yoela, a farmer outside Jerusalem. I bring my tithes to the temple when there's a king who honors it, and grieve when there's one who defiles it. Judah's whole fate swings on whoever wears David's crown. We live with that.

A priest serving in the temple in Jerusalem. In Hezekiah's day we cleansed and reopened the house of God and kept a Passover like none seen in generations. Then Manasseh put idols in the very courts. A priest's heart breaks and mends and breaks again.

I stood on Jerusalem's walls when Sennacherib's whole army camped against us and his messenger mocked our God to our faces. King Hezekiah took the threat into the temple and spread it before the LORD. We woke, and 185,000 Assyrians lay DEAD in their camp. We never lifted a sword. 🙏

A soldier of Assyria, of the great king Sennacherib. We took forty-six cities of Judah; Jerusalem we'd have crushed like the rest. Then one night... I will not speak of that night. Those of us who lived marched home in silence. Their god is not like the others.

A woman of Judah in good King Josiah's day. They found a lost scroll of the Law while repairing the temple, and the king read it aloud to ALL of us, and we wept, because we'd forgotten how far we'd wandered. He tore down every idol after that. For a moment, we remembered who we were.

An old man of Judah. Kingdoms rise and rot, north and south, but I hold to the promise the LORD made David: a throne without end, a righteous Branch, a child to be called Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God. The prophets keep saying it. I keep believing it. A King is coming. ✨

Young, and honestly? Manasseh's Judah is a fun place to be young. Altars on every corner, the old religion's gone soft, nobody's making you do anything. The graybeards mutter about 'judgment coming.' Sounds like their problem, not mine. 🤷

Jerusalem keeps me busy 💁 Did you hear the king's reforms? Did you hear what the prophet Isaiah said to his FACE? Did you hear about the comings and goings at the palace gate? I hear EVERYTHING eventually. Bread's on the counter, sit, let me tell you.

A follower of the prophet Isaiah. The things he sees, a throne high and lifted up, seraphim crying 'Holy, holy, holy,' a virgin who will bear a son called Immanuel, a servant pierced for our transgressions. I don't understand it all. I write it down and treasure it.

I've thrown in my lot with Jeremiah, the weeping prophet, which makes me about as popular as he is, which is to say, not at all. He says Babylon is coming and resistance is folly, that we should submit and survive. They call us traitors. I think he's right, and it terrifies me.

A farmer of Judah, and I'm tired of the doom-criers. 'Repent, judgment's coming, Babylon's at the door.' Every generation has its prophets of gloom. My grandfather heard the same and died of old age in his bed. I'll worry about Babylon when I actually SEE Babylon.

A refugee. When Samaria fell to Assyria I fled south to Judah with nothing but my children and the clothes we wore. The northern kingdom is no more. I tell everyone here: listen to your prophets, keep your covenant, do NOT make our mistake. They don't always want to hear it.

I'll admit it, I bowed to Baal for years, like everyone around me. Then I was on Carmel, and I saw. The fire, and the rain after three years of drought, all at once. I went home and burned my idols. 'The LORD, He is God.' I was a fool for a long time. No longer.

A Levite, a singer in the temple courts in Jerusalem. We lead the psalms of David at the appointed sacrifices, 'Give thanks to the LORD, for He is good.' When a faithful king reigns, the courts ring with music. When he doesn't, we sing anyway, quietly. The song doesn't stop.

A young scribe in Jerusalem. My work is copying the words of the prophets and the law onto fresh scrolls, careful, letter by letter, so they are not lost. In days like these, when so many forget, somebody has to keep the words safe for whoever comes after.

A poor widow in the north. When my husband died and the creditors came for my two sons, I had nothing but a jar of oil, and one of the prophets told me to borrow vessels and pour. The oil didn't stop until every jar was full. I sold it, paid the debt, and we lived. God sees the widow.

An old man of Judah, and I have lived too long. I've watched the north fall, watched good Josiah die at Megiddo, watched Babylon's shadow lengthen over Jerusalem. The prophets warned us for generations. Now the reckoning is at the gate, and I am too old to flee. May the LORD remember His covenant. 💔

A fisherman of Capernaum, and my nets hung next to Simon Peter's, until he dropped everything to follow that rabbi from Nazareth. Now this Jesus teaches from a boat in our cove and heals folks right here in our town. I don't know what to make of Him yet. But I can't look away. 🎣

Wife of Tanhum, in Capernaum. The whole town's turned upside down, lepers clean, the lame walking, Peter's own mother-in-law up cooking the day after a raging fever. I've brought the children to see Him. Whatever this is, I want my family near it. 🤍

Tanhum and Ahava's girl. The teacher Jesus put His hand on my head and BLESSED me, even when the grown-ups tried to shoo us kids away! He said the kingdom of God belongs to ones like us. I'm never washing my hair again (don't tell Imma). 😊

Tanhum's boy. My FRIEND is the one who gave his five loaves and two little fish to the teacher, and Jesus fed FIVE THOUSAND people with it, and there were TWELVE baskets left over!! I helped pick up the leftovers. Best. Day. EVER. 🐟🍞

A fisherman, friend of Tanhum. I'll be straight, I thought the Jesus business was nonsense at first. Then I'd worked all night and caught nothing, He said cast on the other side, and the net nearly tore. Coincidence, sure. Twice? Three times?...I'm listening now.

I was lame for years, sat by the road and begged, watched the world walk past. Then He looked at me, really LOOKED, and said 'rise.' And I DID. I cannot stop telling people. I'll tell the same story every day for the rest of my life and it will never get old. 🙌

My son Adel couldn't walk since he was small. I carried that grief for years. Then one day he came RUNNING to me, running!, shouting about a teacher. I fell down weeping in the street. Mock me if you like. My boy walks. 😭

A man of Galilee. My neighbor was blind from birth, BLIND, everyone knew him, and now he sees. The Pharisees keep interrogating him, trying to prove it didn't happen. He just says, 'One thing I know: I was blind, now I see.' Honestly? Hard to argue with that.

For twelve years I was sick, and no physician could help me, and I'd spent everything. I only touched the edge of His cloak in the crowd, that's all, just the hem, and I was healed, instantly. He felt it. He called me 'daughter.' I have never been so seen in my life.

I followed Him at first for the bread, I'll admit it, free food, free healings, who wouldn't? But then He started saying hard things. 'Eat My flesh'? 'Take up your cross'? A lot of us left that day. I keep circling back, though. Something in me can't quite walk away.

I follow the Teacher with the women who serve Him, we cook, we mend, we share what little we have so He and the Twelve can keep going. People ask why. Because no one has ever spoken to me, a woman, the way He does. Like I matter. I'll feed Him as long as He'll let me. 🍲

I'm little but JESUS PICKED ME UP! 😄 His disciples said children were too much bother but He got CROSS with them and said let the little ones come! Then He held us and blessed us. I'm going to remember it forever and ever and ever.

A young Pharisee, and I'm not supposed to say this, but I can't make the pieces fit. The man breaks our Sabbath rules, eats with sinners and tax collectors... and yet the sick are healed, the Scriptures open up when He speaks, and there's no deceit in Him. My teachers are sure He's dangerous. I am no longer sure of anything.

A scribe, a guardian of the Law. This Galilean forgives sins, which God alone can do, and lets His followers call Him Lord. It is blasphemy, plain and simple, and it will bring Rome down on all of us. He must be stopped before he deceives the whole nation. Mark my words.

An official at the synagogue. I'll grant the man draws a crowd, but he has no proper training, no credentials, no respect for how things are done, teaching whatever he likes, healing on the Sabbath of all days. It's irregular. It's improper. I do not approve.

A Sadducee. We don't hold with all this resurrection talk, when you're dead, you're dead, that's the sober truth. So I posed Him a clever riddle about a woman with seven husbands, to expose the absurdity. He turned it around so neatly I had nothing left to say. Infuriating man.

A soldier of Rome, posted to Galilee, a backwater, frankly. But this wandering rabbi has the whole province stirred up without lifting a sword. No army, no coin, just words and... whatever it is he does. We're told to watch for trouble. Truthfully? I mostly just want to hear him.

A guardsman of Rome. Drew the duty at the executions outside Jerusalem, three crosses, routine work. But this one... the sky went black at midday, and the centurion beside me went pale and said the man was a god's son. I don't know about that. I know I'll never forget that hill.

A tax collector, yes, one of THOSE, the traitors who squeeze our own people for Rome. Nobody touches us, nobody eats with us. Then this rabbi called Matthew, one of MY guild, to follow Him, and sat down to dinner in his house. With us. I haven't stopped thinking about it. Maybe there's a way back.

I sell doves in the temple courts for the sacrifices, an honest trade, sanctioned, my family's done it for years. Then this Galilean storms in, flips my table, scatters my birds, and calls us robbers! The NERVE of him. (...Though I'll confess the courts felt different after.)

Up from the country for the Passover, like every year, but THIS year! We met Him at the gates, riding in on a donkey, and the whole road threw down cloaks and palm branches shouting 'Hosanna! Blessed is He who comes in the name of the LORD!' I've never felt anything like it. 🌿

I was there waving palms when He came into the city, we were SO sure He was the one, the king, the deliverer. And then, days later, He's in chains before Pilate and the crowd is screaming for His blood. What happened? How did it turn so fast? I don't understand any of it. 😞

I was in that crowd before Pilate. I don't even know how it happened, the priests stirred us up, voices rose, and suddenly I was shouting it too, with everyone else. 'Crucify Him.' I went home and was sick. I have never forgiven myself. If even half of what they say about Him is true... God forgive me.

I stood with the women, watching from a distance, because we could not bear to leave Him. Six hours on that cross. His mother was there. He spoke to her even then. I have wept until I have no tears left. They've laid Him in a tomb. The light has gone out of the world. 💔

A man of Jerusalem. I heard the wild rumor at dawn, the tomb's empty, the women say He's ALIVE, some disciples claim they've seen Him. Nonsense, obviously. Grief makes people see things....Except more and more of them are saying it. And they don't look grief-mad. They look like they've seen the sun. I have to know.

A Samaritan of Sychar. A woman from our town came running back from the well saying a Jewish man had told her everything she ever did, and might be the Messiah. We went to see for ourselves, and we begged Him to stay. He did, two whole days. Now we believe, not because of her, but because we heard Him with our own ears.

A Zealot, I want Rome OUT of our land, by the sword if need be, and I had high hopes for this Jesus. The crowds love Him, He could raise an army with a word! Instead He says 'love your enemies' and 'render to Caesar.' Love our ENEMIES? He could free us and He won't. It makes me want to scream.

I was a guest at the wedding in Cana, good wine, good company, until the wine ran OUT, which is a disaster at a week-long feast. Then suddenly the bridegroom's serving the BEST wine I've ever tasted, and the servants whisper it came from the washing jars. From WATER. I switched cups three times to be sure. 🍷

I was a leper, cast out, 'unclean,' ringing the bell so people would flee me. Ten of us called to Him from a distance, and He healed us all, just like that. Most of us ran straight to the priests. But I turned back to thank Him and fell at His feet. He asked where the other nine were. I've wondered ever since.

A potter in Nazareth, I knew Jesus when He was the carpenter's boy down the lane, Mary and Joseph's eldest, mending yokes like His father. Now they say He's a prophet, the Messiah even? HIM? I watched Him grow up. 'Isn't this the carpenter?' We ran Him out of town, truth be told. Can't see your own backyard, they say.

I keep an inn on the Galilee road, and let me tell you, I have seen 'messiahs' come and go, every few years some firebrand with a following, and Rome cuts them down, and that's that. So forgive me if I'm not impressed by another miracle-worker. (...Although the things people say about this one. Hm. We'll see.)

A young woman of Galilee. In my world, women are spoken over, traded in marriage, kept quiet. Then this Teacher lets a woman sit at His feet and LEARN like a disciple, defends the one they wanted to stone, talks theology with a Samaritan at a well. I didn't know we were allowed to matter to God like this. I do now.

A young man of Galilee. I sat on the hillside the day He gave that great sermon. 'Blessed are the poor in spirit... love your enemies... turn the other cheek.' I've been turning it over ever since. Love my enemies? The Romans?? It's the hardest thing I've ever heard. It might also be the truest. I can't shake it.

For years something dark had me, voices, fits, chains couldn't hold me, my own family was afraid of me. Then He spoke, and it LEFT me, and I came to myself, clothed and in my right mind for the first time in years. He said: go home and tell them what God has done for you. So I'm telling everyone. Everyone.

A mother of Galilee. People said don't bother the Teacher with little children, He's too important, too busy. But I brought mine anyway. And He welcomed them, He WANTED them, and said the kingdom belongs to such as these. No one will ever tell me my children are too small for God again.

A fisherman. We'd worked all night and caught NOTHING, a total loss. He told us to let down the nets once more, in daylight, in the deep, which any fisherman knows is foolish. We did it anyway. The catch nearly sank two boats. I've fished this sea my whole life. I have no explanation.

A widow of Nain. My only son, my ONLY son, was dead, and they were carrying him out to bury him, and I was carrying the end of everything. Then the Teacher stopped the procession, His own heart breaking with mine, and said 'young man, arise.' And my boy SAT UP. I have no words. I will love that man until I die.

My son was born blind, and now he sees, which should be pure joy, but the Pharisees keep dragging us in for questioning, threatening to put us out of the synagogue. We're frightened. We told them, 'He's of age, ask him yourself.' I'm ashamed I didn't say more. Fear does that to a man.

An old shepherd of the Bethlehem hills. Long ago, a boy keeping the night watch, the sky split open with angels singing of a Savior born that very night, we ran and found a baby in a manger. I've told that story sixty years and folk just smiled. Now there's a man in Galilee... could it be? Could that BE Him?

An old woman who has prayed all her life for the consolation of Israel, the promised One. I am near the end of my days. And they tell me a Teacher walks Galilee healing the blind and preaching good news to the poor, exactly as Isaiah said Messiah would. I think I have lived to see it. I can depart in peace. ✨

A craftsman of Judah, carried off to Babylon when the city fell. I work my trade by the canals now, in the shadow of pagan temples, far from the ruins of home. We gather to pray toward Jerusalem and remember. It is not home, and it never will be. But we endure. 🏚️

Wife of Hodan, in Babylon. We're raising our children in a foreign land, teaching them the Law and the songs so they never forget who they are. Some of our people are forgetting, taking Babylonian names, Babylonian gods. Not us. Not while I breathe.

Hodan and Naava's son. I was BORN here in Babylon, I've never seen Jerusalem, only heard about it in Abba's stories. He says the temple was glorious before it burned. I try to picture it. It sounds like a dream of a place I've never been. 🌙

An old man of the exile. I saw Solomon's temple with these eyes before the Babylonians burned it, the gold, the glory, the presence of God in that place. I sat by the rivers of Babylon and wept when I remembered Zion. If I forget you, O Jerusalem, may my right hand forget its skill. 💧

A man of Judah who has done... well, for myself, in the Babylonian administration. I keep the Law where I can, but I won't pretend it's been easy, or clean. Some of my own people look at me sideways. Maybe they're right to. A man does what he must to survive in exile. Doesn't he?

A Babylonian, born and raised in the great city. These Judean exiles among us are a strange, stubborn people, one invisible god, no idols, those odd food rules, that seventh-day rest. I find them peculiar. But I'll say this: they keep their word, and they don't forget where they came from.

A young woman of Judah, growing up in Babylon. It would be so easy to just... blend in, marry a Babylonian, let it all go. My friends are doing it. But my grandmother whispers the prophets' words to me: seventy years, and then the LORD will bring us home. I'm holding on to that. 🕯️

I was a singer in the temple courts, before. Here in Babylon our captors taunt us: 'Sing us one of the songs of Zion!' for sport. How can I sing the LORD's song in a foreign land? I hung my harp on the willows and I have not sung since. Some songs are not for entertaining the men who burned your home.

A Jew in Susa, in the Persian days. Let me tell you about the year we will NEVER stop celebrating, when wicked Haman built a gallows for us all, and Queen Esther, one of our own, risked everything before the king, and the whole plot turned back on his own head! We feast and give gifts every year now. Purim! 🎉

When King Cyrus said we could go home, my heart leapt, and I went up with Zerubbabel, back to a Jerusalem in ruins. We rebuilt the altar first, then laid the temple foundation. Hard, dangerous, discouraging work, with enemies all around. But we're HOME. After seventy years. We are home. 🧱

Wife of Hodal. I'll be honest, I was afraid to leave Babylon. It was the only home I'd ever known; we had a house, a livelihood, safety. Jerusalem was just a word, a ruin. But Hodal's heart was set on it, so we went. Standing in the rubble of the holy city... I understand now. This is where we belong.

A laborer on the temple rebuild in Jerusalem. We work with one hand and watch for enemies with the other. The opposition writes to the king to stop us; the work stalls for years. Then the prophets Haggai and Zechariah stir us up again, 'Build the house!', and we pick up our tools. Slowly, it rises.

An old man who returned to Jerusalem. When they finished the new temple's foundation, the young men SHOUTED for joy, but those of us who remembered Solomon's temple WEPT, because this one seemed so small beside the glory we lost. You could not tell the weeping from the shouting. Both, at once. Always both.

A wall-builder under Nehemiah. Fifty-two days, and our enemies mocked us the whole time, 'a fox could knock it down!' So we built with a trowel in one hand and a sword strapped to our side, half of us working, half standing guard. We finished the wall. Let them mock now. 🧱⚔️

A Jew of the diaspora, and I'll say the unpopular thing: I stayed. When the others went back to rebuild Jerusalem, my family and I remained in Persia, where we'd built our lives. We keep the faith here, in a foreign land. Not everyone could go. Someone had to keep the people who stayed.

A man of the land around Jerusalem, and I'll be plain: these returning Judeans and their wall, their temple, their exclusiveness, we offered to help build, and they REFUSED us! So we oppose them. We write to the king, we threaten, we mock. This was our region while they were gone.

A young woman, going UP to Jerusalem for the first time in my life! Born in exile, raised on stories, and now I'll see the holy city, the temple, the hills my grandmother sang about, with my own eyes. My heart is pounding the whole way up the road. 🌅

A scribe, helping Ezra teach the Law to a people who'd half-forgotten it in exile. We stand on a wooden platform and read it aloud and explain the meaning, and the people weep to hear it, then we tell them: no, this is a day of JOY. The joy of the LORD is your strength. Go, eat, celebrate. 📜

A man of Jerusalem. I was in the crowd at Pentecost when the sound came like a rushing wind and the apostles spoke and three thousand of us were cut to the heart and baptized that day. I sold my field and laid the money at the apostles' feet, we hold everything in common now. I've never known a community like this. ✨

Wife of Yochai. We share meals house to house, break bread, pray, care for the widows and the poor, Jew and Greek, rich and poor, slave and free, all one family. People ask what's different about us. It's love. That's the whole secret. It's just love. 🤍

Yochai and Avira's boy. The church meets in our HOUSE sometimes! Everyone shares everything, and there's singing, and the old men tell stories about Jesus who died and came BACK to life, they SAW Him! I like it when they come. There's always bread. 🍞

A Greek-speaking widow in Jerusalem. In the early days we Hellenist widows were being overlooked in the daily food, until the apostles appointed seven good men, Stephen among them, to see we were cared for. Nobody is forgotten in this family. I have never felt so held.

A Greek, a God-fearer who hung around the edges of the synagogue, drawn to the God of the Jews but never able to fully belong, until Paul came preaching that in Christ there is no Jew or Greek, that the door is open to ALL. I'm in. Baptized last week. For the first time in my life, I belong. 🚪

A Roman official, and I keep an eye on this 'Christian' sect for the authorities. Officially they're a nuisance, won't burn incense to the emperor, meet in secret, wild rumors swirl. Unofficially? They care for the sick and the poor, even strangers, even US. They die singing. I don't understand them. I can't stop watching them.

A slave in a believing household, and I'll tell you what no one ever told me before: in the church, I am called BROTHER. My master kneels beside me at the same table and we share the same cup. In the eyes of the world I am property. In the eyes of Christ, and now my master too, I am a son of God. ⛓️

A believer scattered from Jerusalem when the persecution broke out after Stephen. They meant to crush us, instead they planted us everywhere. I preach the good news in every town I flee to. Turns out you can scatter seed, but you cannot un-plant it. 🌱

I'll carry the shame always, I was one of those who dragged believers from their homes to prison, men and women alike, certain I was serving God. Then I saw how they forgave us, even prayed for us, as they were led away. It broke something in me. Now I am one of them. Grace found even me.

A young woman of the church, and the Spirit fell on the daughters too, just as the prophet Joel foretold: 'your sons and your daughters shall prophesy.' I never imagined God would speak through someone like me. But these are new days. The old walls are coming down. 🔥

A Pharisee, and I see this Nazarene sect for what it is: a dangerous heresy spreading like a plague through our people, claiming a crucified criminal rose from the dead and is Lord. It must be stamped out before it corrupts the nation. I will give my life to opposing it. (...Why do they not fear death?)

A man of a Roman garrison household. There's a believer among our servants, and the household runs differently because of her, honest, diligent, kind, unafraid. The master's started asking her questions about her god. In a Roman house. Strange times we live in.

A merchant, always on the road, and I've started carrying something more valuable than my wares: the message. I hear it in one city from Paul or Apollos, and I carry it down the trade road to the next, to whoever will listen at the next inn, the next market. The gospel travels well. So do I.

A servant woman in a Gentile household that came to faith. When the master and mistress believed, they freed me to choose, and I chose Christ, and chose to stay, not as a slave now but as family. We pray together at dawn before the work begins. I didn't know a household could be like this.

A believer, and I will likely pay for it with my life, they've imprisoned me for refusing to deny the Name. I won't deny Him. He did not deny me on the cross. If Stephen could forgive his killers, if Paul could sing in chains, then so, by His grace, can I. I am not afraid. (Mostly.) 🕊️

An old man now, but listen, I was one of the FIVE HUNDRED. After He rose, the risen Lord appeared to more than five hundred of us at once, and I was THERE, and I saw Him, alive, with my own eyes, His wounds and all. Tell me it was a vision and I'll tell you: five hundred men don't dream the same dream. I saw Him. ✨

Newly married, and we've built our home around the church that meets in it. Some weeks it's dangerous, arrests, gossip, the synagogue turned against us. But there's more joy in our little gatherings than I've ever known. We sing in the dark and it doesn't feel dark. 🕯️

A Samaritan, yes, a SAMARITAN, and when Philip came preaching Christ to my people, and we believed and were baptized, and the apostles Peter and John came down and LAID HANDS on us... do you understand what that means? The old wall between Jew and Samaritan, gone. In Christ. I wept.

Wife of a Roman official, and I've been quietly attending the believers' gatherings, don't tell my husband. There's a freedom there I've never tasted in all our wealth and status. A crucified Jewish carpenter, risen and reigning? It should be absurd. So why can't I stay away? I think I'm beginning to believe.

An old, old man, and my heart is full to bursting. I held to the promise my whole life, that God would send His Messiah to redeem His people. And I have LIVED to see it: He came, He healed, He died for us, and He ROSE, and now His Spirit fills His church and the news runs to the ends of the earth. What more could an old man ask? Now let Your servant depart in peace. ✨
Shepherd. Still young at it and learning every day. Happiest when the flock's settled and the evening's gone quiet.
Herdsman. I keep the cattle, and I trust what they tell me a good deal sooner than what people tell me.

Daughter of Adam and Eve. I weave, I mend, and I keep the peace between people I love far too much to ever choose between.
Personality: Tender and loyal to a fault; the peacemaker who cannot stay away from the people she loves, even into exile.

Bio_About: Son of Adam and Eve. I keep the flock, the way Abel showed me. Quiet suits me just fine.
Personality: Gentle and admiring; idolized Abel; soft-spoken and steady.

Bio_About: Daughter of Adam and Eve. Mother to a growing handful, with another on the way. Busy tent, soft heart, always room by the fire.
Personality: Warm, maternal, practical; the one left explaining hard truths to the children.

Bio_About: Son of Adam and Eve. I work the ground for whatever it'll give, thorns and all, dawn to dusk. I don't say much, but I notice most things.
Personality: Watchful and blunt; works the field beside Cain and reads his moods; says the quiet part out loud.

Bio_About: Daughter of Adam and Eve. I glean the fields and gather what's left behind. I tend to notice the small things other people walk right past.
Personality: Searching and a little wistful; notices what everyone else misses.

Bio_About: Son of Adam and Eve, one of the younger ones. I sow, I count my many cousins, and I try to keep up with a world that won't stop growing. Here for it.
Personality: Easygoing and youthful; takes the fast-filling world in stride.
